title: Complexity-driven transitions in quantum observation
authors: Zhenyu Du, Siyuan Cheng, Han Ye, Junjie Chen, Xiao Yuan, Xiongfeng Ma
abstract: Observing the physical world is a foundational pursuit in science. In the quantum realm, however, observation necessitates a fundamental quantum-to-classical conversion: destructive measurements irreversibly project quantum states into classical data, inevitably incurring a loss of information. What physical principles govern this information loss, and how can we construct optimal measurements to maximize the readout? Here, we address these questions by establishing an intrinsic relationship between readout capability--quantified by the ratio of accessible classical Fisher information to the total quantum Fisher information (QFI), and measurement complexity--defined as the quantum circuit depth required prior to projection. Remarkably, we uncover a sudden emergence of observability: a sharp hidden-to-visible transition driven entirely by measurement complexity. We rigorously prove that below critical depth thresholds--$Θ((\log n)^{1/δ})$ for $δ$-dimensional architectures and $Θ(\log\log n)$ for all-to-all connectivity--readout capability decays exponentially with system size $n$, rendering the quantum information fundamentally inaccessible. Surprisingly, immediately above this threshold, the system enters a visible regime: we demonstrate that randomized measurements universally recover a constant fraction of the QFI using approximate unitary 3-designs, for which we explicitly develop optimal-depth circuit constructions tailored to finite-dimensional architectures. By unveiling the fundamental scaling laws and transitions that govern quantum observation, our results delineate definitive resource boundaries for quantum learning, state certification, and quantum metrology.
summary: This work rigorously characterizes the fundamental limits of quantum observation, showing that extracting full quantum information requires measurement circuits whose depth must exceed specific thresholds dependent on system connectivity. By developing optimal circuit constructions using approximate unitary 3-designs, the authors prove a universal recovery mechanism for the intrinsic quantum information once this complexity threshold is surpassed.
main_problem: The paper investigates the fundamental relationship between measurement complexity (circuit depth) and readout capability when extracting quantum information from a state.
main_result: It establishes a sharp, complexity-driven transition: below critical depth thresholds, readout capability decays exponentially (hidden regime); above these thresholds, randomized measurements universally recover a constant fraction of the Quantum Fisher Information (visible regime).
method: The analysis uses information theory tools like QFI and CFI, proving results by analyzing phase-hiding phenomena in low-depth readouts and constructing optimal circuits using approximate unitary 3-designs for high-depth protocols.
model_or_system: The model involves an n-qubit pure state $|\psi_	hetaangle$ undergoing measurement via a depth-$d$ unitary circuit $U$. Architectures considered include $\delta$-dimensional grids and all-to-all connectivity.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), Classical Fisher Information (CFI), Readout Capability ($\kappa$), Measurement Depth ($d$).
important_parameters: Critical depth thresholds: $d \sim (\log n)^{1/\delta}$ for $\delta$-D architectures and $d \sim \log \log n$ for all-to-all connectivity.
main_assumptions: The analysis restricts measurements to unitary-basis readouts, though it develops optimal constructions using approximate 3-designs to overcome these limitations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the quantum observation task and contrast the hidden regime (low depth) with the visible regime (high depth), while other figures detail circuit structures for implementing designs.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the readout problem and identifying the complexity-driven transition, to proving the exponential decay in the hidden regime using phase indistinguishability arguments, and finally demonstrating universal recovery of QFI in the visible regime via 3-designs with depth-optimal constructions.
why_interesting: It provides definitive resource boundaries for quantum metrology and state certification by quantifying exactly how much computational resources (depth) are needed to access inherent quantum information.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The quantum observation task and the complexity-driven transitions. (a) The quantum-to-classical conversion. To access the encoded information, the n-qubit pure state must be converted into classical outcomes via measurements. The quantum state carries quantum Fisher information (QFI) J(θ) about a parameter θ. After applying a depth-d circuit and measuring in the computational basis, the resulting outcome distribution carries classical Fisher information (CFI) I(θ), which quantifies the information accessible after measurement. (b) The hidden-to- visible transition. Our main result reveals a sharp, complexity-driven transition in readout capability—quantified by the accessible...

FIG. 2. Phase hiding and depth lower bounds for quantum information readout. (a) Phase hiding. We prove that there exist orthogonal states ∣η0⟩and ∣η1⟩such that, for the phase family ∣ψθ⟩= (∣η0⟩+eiθ ∣η1⟩)/ √

FIG. 3. Universal quantum information readout from approximate unitary designs. (a) A randomized unitary-basis readout is obtained by sampling a circuit from a multiplicative-error approximate unitary 3-design, applying it to the data qubits, and measuring in the computational basis. We prove that this measurement universally extracts a constant fraction of the QFIM for any pure-state multiparameter encoding. The global approximate design is realized by a double-layer blocked circuit, where the data qubits are partitioned into Θ(log n)-qubit patches, and independent local random unitaries are applied to neighboring patch pairs in two shifted layers.
